title: 10 best herbal teas for taste changes

remedy_properties: Digestive relief: Peppermint tea's menthol content can relax digestive muscles and reduce inflammation, helping to alleviate symptoms like nausea and bloating associated with taste changes.

Antioxidant activity: Peppermint tea contains antioxidants that neutralize free radicals, which can damage taste buds and affect the sense of taste, helping to preserve and restore normal taste function.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The flavonoids present in peppermint tea have anti-inflammatory effects, which can help reduce inflammation in the mouth, throat, and digestive tract, making it easier to experience flavors and textures normally.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Mentha x piperita, also known as peppermint, teas helps with taste changes because it has a cooling and refreshing effect on the palate. The menthol content in peppermint helps to reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, which can cause changes in taste. Additionally, peppermint's strong, invigorating flavor can help mask unpleasant tastes and odors, making it easier to eat and drink. This can be particularly beneficial for individuals experiencing taste changes due to illness, medication, or other underlying conditions.
remedy_constituents: Menthol: Helps to reduce inflammation and irritation in the mouth and throat, relieving symptoms associated with taste changes such as dry mouth or a burning sensation.
Menthone: Exhibits analgesic and anti-inflammatory properties, which can help alleviate pain and discomfort in the mouth and throat that may be contributing to taste changes.

Rosmarinic acid: Acts as an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent, which can help protect the mucous membranes in the mouth and throat from damage and promote healing, potentially improving taste perception.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Peppermint leaves are commonly used to make teas due to their strong, refreshing flavor and digestive benefits.
Stems: Some peppermint teas also include the stems for added flavor and a slightly bitter taste, which helps balance the sweetness of the leaves.
Roots: The roots of peppermint, though less commonly used, can be utilized to create a more earthy and robust tea with deeper flavor profiles.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 20-30 fresh mentha x piperita leaves from a well-maintained herb garden in the morning.
Rinse the harvested leaves under cold running water to remove dirt and debris thoroughly.
Chop the rinsed mentha x piperita leaves into small pieces weighing about 2-3 grams per serving.
Steep 1-2 teaspoons of chopped mentha x piperita leaves in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea mixture through a fine-mesh sieve into a cup and discard the solids immediately.

remedy_properties: Antioxidant properties: Lemon balm tea's high antioxidant content helps protect the tongue and mouth from free radical damage, which can contribute to taste changes caused by oxidative stress.

Anti-inflammatory properties: The anti-inflammatory compounds in lemon balm tea can reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, alleviating symptoms of taste changes such as dry mouth, burning sensations, or a metallic taste.
Soothing and calming effects: Lemon balm tea's calming properties can help reduce stress and anxiety, which are common triggers for taste changes such as aversion to certain foods or flavors.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Melissa officinalis, also known as lemon balm, teas helps with taste changes because of its calming and soothing properties. The herb contains rosmarinic acid and other compounds that can help reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, which may be contributing to taste changes. Additionally, lemon balm tea has been traditionally used to alleviate digestive issues and stress, both of which can impact taste perception. Its antioxidant properties may also help to protect the taste buds and support overall oral health.
remedy_constituents: Linalool: This terpene has been shown to have anxiolytic properties, which can help reduce anxiety-related taste changes such as metallic or bitter tastes.
Limonene: As a terpene, limonene has been found to have anti-inflammatory properties that can help alleviate taste changes caused by inflammation in the mouth and throat.

Rosmarinic acid: This phenolic compound has been shown to have antioxidant properties that can help protect against oxidative stress, which can contribute to taste changes such as bitterness or metallic tastes.
remedy_parts: Leaves: Used to make teas due to their high concentration of citral, which gives a refreshing and lemony flavor.
Flowers: Utilized in teas for their sweet and slightly bitter taste, adding a floral and delicate flavor profile.
Roots: Employed in teas to provide a more robust and earthy flavor, with a slightly sweet undertone.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2-3 tablespoons of melissa officinalis leaves and flowers from a well-maintained garden or a reliable supplier.
Rinse the leaves and flowers with cold water to remove dirt and debris then gently pat dry.
Steep 1 teaspoon of dried melissa officinalis leaves in boiling water for 5-7 minutes to release flavors and oils.
Strain the tea into a cup using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth to remove solids.
Serve the melissa officinalis tea hot or iced within 30 minutes to 1 hour of preparation.

remedy_properties: Digestive enzymes: Ginger's digestive enzymes, such as zingibain, help to break down proteins and reduce inflammation in the digestive tract, which can alleviate nausea and loss of appetite associated with chemotherapy or radiation treatment.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: Ginger contains anti-inflammatory compounds like gingerol and shogaol that have been shown to reduce inflammation and pain in the mouth and throat, making it easier to eat and drink after a taste change diagnosis.
Antioxidants: Ginger's antioxidants help to protect the body against free radicals that can damage healthy cells and tissues, which can contribute to changes in taste perception.
remedies_list.remedy_desc: Zingiber officinale, also known as ginger, teas helps with taste changes because of its ability to stimulate digestive enzymes and improve nutrient absorption. The active compounds in ginger, such as gingerols and shogaols, have anti-inflammatory properties that can help soothe an irritated tongue and mouth, reducing discomfort associated with taste changes. Additionally, ginger's warm, spicy flavor can help stimulate the appetite and improve the overall experience of eating, making it easier to enjoy food despite taste changes.
remedy_constituents: Gingerols: Gingerols, specifically 6-gingerol and 8-gingerol, help alleviate taste changes by reducing inflammation in the mouth and throat, which can be a result of chemotherapy or radiation therapy, and also by inhibiting the growth of bacteria that can cause bad breath.

Shogaols: Shogaols, particularly 6-shogaol, aid in taste changes by exhibiting anti-inflammatory properties that can help reduce the burning sensation in the mouth and throat, which is often experienced by cancer patients undergoing chemotherapy or radiation therapy.

Zingiberene: Zingiberene, a sesquiterpene, helps alleviate taste changes by exhibiting antioxidant properties that can help protect the oral cavity from oxidative stress and inflammation, which can lead to taste disturbances.
remedy_parts: Rhyzomes: They are used to make teas because of their distinct, warm, and spicy flavor that is commonly associated with ginger.
Roots: They are used to make teas because of their earthy and slightly sweet flavor, which can add depth to herbal blends.
Leaves: They are used to make teas because of their light and citrusy flavor, which can provide a refreshing contrast to the warmth of ginger.
remedy_recipe: Harvest the roots of zingiber officinale in the fall when they are mature and the foliage has begun to die back.
Wash the roots thoroughly with clean water to remove any dirt or debris.
Cut the roots into small pieces and dry them in a low-temperature oven at 150 degrees fahrenheit for 24 hours.
Combine one teaspoon of dried root with 8 ounces of boiling water and steep for 5 to 10 minutes.
Strain the tea and discard the solids before serving to enjoy the medicinal properties of zingiber officinale.

remedy_properties: Antioxidants: Roselle tea contains high levels of antioxidants, which can help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the body, potentially alleviating unpleasant taste changes associated with certain medical conditions.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: The anti-inflammatory properties of Roselle tea may help alleviate symptoms such as dry mouth, burning sensations, or altered taste perceptions commonly experienced during chemotherapy or other medical treatments.

Digestive benefits: Roselle tea has been traditionally used to soothe digestive issues like indigestion and bloating, which can also contribute to changes in taste perception; its potential anti-inflammatory effects on the gut may also help restore normal gut function.

Quercetin: Quercetin, a flavonoid, helps with taste changes by acting as an antioxidant and anti-inflammatory agent, which can reduce inflammation and irritation in the mouth and improve overall taste sensation.
remedy_parts: Flowers: Used to make teas due to their sweet and fruity flavor, which is often used in herbal teas and beverages.
Leaves: Used to make teas due to their slightly bitter taste and earthy flavor, which is often used in medicinal teas and infusions.
Fruits: Used to make teas due to their sweet and slightly tart flavor, which is often used in fruity and refreshing herbal teas.
remedy_recipe: Harvest 2 cups of dried hibiscus sabdariffa flowers from the plant when the flowers are fully open.
Steep 1 tablespoon of dried hibiscus sabdariffa flowers in 1 quart of boiling water for 5 to 10 minutes.
Strain the herbal mixture through a cheesecloth or fine-mesh sieve into a large pitcher.
Add 1 tablespoon of sugar or honey to the herbal infusion and stir until dissolved slowly.
Chill the tea in the refrigerator for at least 2 hours before serving over ice cubes.

Matricine: Matricine, a sesquiterpene lactone, has anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties that may help reduce inflammation and oxidative stress in the mouth and throat, improving taste perception.

remedy_recipe: Harvest 2 to 3 cups of fresh matricaria chamomilla flowers in the morning after the dew has dried.
Rinse the flowers with filtered water to remove any dirt or debris from the environment.
Combine 1 teaspoon of dried flowers or 2 to 3 teaspoons of fresh flowers with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for 5 to 7 minutes to allow the active ingredients to infuse into the water.

remedy_recipe: Gather 2 tablespoons of rosa rubiginosa flowers and 1 teaspoon of dried root bark for tea.
Steep the rosa rubiginosa flowers in 8 ounces of boiling water for 5-7 minutes.
Strain the tea and combine it with the dried root bark steeped in 4 ounces of boiling water for 2-3 minutes.
Combine the two infusions and let it steep for an additional 2-3 minutes.
Strain the tea and serve it immediately in a large cup with honey to taste.

Anti-inflammatory compounds: Elder tea contains anti-inflammatory compounds like sambucus nigra extract, which can help reduce inflammation in the mouth and throat, thereby improving taste sensations and reducing discomfort associated with taste changes.

Soothing demulcents: Elder tea's soothing properties come from its mucilage content, a thick, protective substance that helps calm irritated mucus membranes in the oral cavity, providing relief from dryness, itchiness, and soreness associated with taste changes.
remedy_parts: Roots: Rich in mucilage, which helps soothe and calm digestive issues related to taste changes.
Bark: Containing tannins, which can help reduce inflammation and alleviate bitter or unpleasant tastes.
remedy_recipe: Gather one tablespoon of dried sambucus nigra flowers per eight ounces of water for brewing.
Combine the sambucus nigra flowers with boiling water in a teapot or infuser.
Steep the mixture for 5-7 minutes to allow the flavors and nutrients to combine.
Strain the tea into a cup using a piece of cheesecloth or a fine-mesh sieve.
Drink the tea immediately, adjusting the sweetness with honey if desired.

remedy_recipe: Harvest fresh achillea millefolium leaves and flowers or purchase dried ones from a reputable source.

Combine one teaspoon of dried herbs or two teaspoons of fresh herbs with 8 ounces of boiling water.
Steep the mixture for 5 to 10 minutes to allow the herbal compounds to infuse into the liquid.
Strain the tea using a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth and discard the solids.

Strain the tea through a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into another cup, discarding the solids.

remedy_recipe: Rinse dried angelica sinensis roots thoroughly under cold running water to remove impurities.

Strain the tea leaves with a fine-mesh sieve or cheesecloth into a large cup or teapot.

supplementary_causes: Ailments similar to taste changes that are treated with herbal teas are digestive issues such as bloating, constipation, and indigestion. Herbal teas like peppermint, ginger, and chamomile help soothe the stomach and alleviate symptoms of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS). Additionally, herbal teas like echinacea, slippery elm, and licorice root can treat throat infections, sore throats, and coughs by reducing inflammation and promoting healing.
intro_desc: Herbal teas for taste changes are plant-based infusions used to alleviate alterations in taste perception, often caused by medications, health conditions, or aging. These teas help restore a person's natural taste by combating taste changes. The best herbal teas for taste changes include Zingiber officinale, Glycyrrhiza glabra, Ginkgo biloba, Rosmarinus officinalis, Cinnamomum verum, Curcuma longa, Melissa officinalis, Foeniculum vulgare, Thymus vulgaris, and Silybum marianum. If left untreated, taste changes can lead to social isolation, poor nutrition, and decreased quality of life, as individuals struggle to enjoy food and interact with others.
supplementary_best_treatment: The best combination of herbal teas that help with taste changes is a blend of peppermint, ginger, and licorice root tea. Peppermint tea helps to stimulate saliva production, while ginger tea aids in digestion and reduces inflammation in the mouth. Licorice root tea, rich in antioxidants, soothes and calms the digestive tract. Drinking a combination of these teas can help restore balance to taste buds and alleviate taste changes caused by illness, medication, or other factors. This blend promotes overall oral health and well-being.
intro_study: For example, according to a study published by Journal of food science and technology, researchers conducted an experiment to develop herbal tea from Bacopa monnieri with known health benefits. They combined Bacopa monnieri with different spices or herbal ingredients, and found that the pepper-brahmi combination had a higher sensory score for taste, with a score of 8.43. This suggests that herbal teas can be tailored to have a desirable taste, which may be beneficial for individuals experiencing taste changes, such as those with taste disorders or individuals undergoing chemotherapy, who may have a reduced sense of taste.
